utterances: We Fada wa dey een heaben, leh everybody honor your name.
We pray that soon ya gwine rule over de world.
Wasoneba ting ya wahn, leh um be so in dis world, same like dey in heaven.
Give we the food what we need dis day, yah, an eb'ry(every)day.
Forgive we for we sin, cause we da' forgive dey what do bad to we.
Let we don't hard tests wen Satan try we.
Keep we from evil.
That's the Lord's Prayer in Gullah.
This is NPR News.
While the diplomatic discussions about Iran continue, along with the sanctions on nuclear technology, Russia has gone ahead with the sale of new conventional weapons.
Moscow has sold Iran anti-aircraft missiles, as NPR's Gregory Feifer reports from the Russian capital.
Defense Minister Sergei Ivanov announced the fulfillment of Moscow's billion dollar deal with Iran to reporters on Tuesday.
(Speaking foreign language)
We have supplied a modern short-ranged Tor-M1 anti-aircraft missile systems in accordance with our contract, he said.
Iran is not under international sanctions.
The deal has angered Washington, which accuses Iran of being the world's leading state sponsor of terrorism.
Tehran is suspected of developing a secret nuclear arms program and is under U.N.
sanctions against atomic materials and technology.
That hasn't stopped Russia from building a nuclear power plant in Iran, which says it's only interested in atomic energy for peaceful purposes.
Defense Minister Ivanov also said this week that Moscow may supply Tehran with more weapons in the future.
(Speaking foreign language)
If Iran wants to buy defensive - I underline defensive - equipments for its armed forces, he said, then why not.
Tehran's recently acquired Tor-M1 missiles are highly advance battlefield weapons mounted on armored vehicles.
They were designed by the Soviet Union to protect tank and infantry formations in the conflict with NATO forces in Europe.
Defense analyst Pavel Felgenhauer said the missiles are offensive, not defensive weapons.

This is a serious development that gives the Iranian military a possibility that they can sort of believe that they can withstand, to some extent, American air supremacy in any kind of clash on the battlefield.
Iran isn't the only U.S.
opponent Russia supplies with weapons.
Moscow became the largest arms provider to developing countries last year.
Its biggest customers are India and China.
But Russia also sells weapons to countries like Venezuela and Syria.
Israel has accused Syria of supplying Russian rockets to Hezbollah, something Moscow denies.
Critics also accused Russia of failing to prevent its arms from getting into the hands of warlords in Africa.
Victor Komardin is deputy head of the main state arms export company, Rosoboron Export.
He denies accusations Russia is responsible for its weapons getting into the wrong hands.
(Through translator) Everything that's blamed on us isn't our fault.
All those Kalashnikov automatic rifles, grenades, ammunitions - all that's counterfeit.
Komardin says Moscow's arms exports are simply a legitimate business.
Rosoboron Export was created to administer sales from various Russian manufacturers.
That way, we can oversee our exports, compile statistics and allow the government to make sure it's fulfilling its international obligations.
But defense analyst Alexander Golts says Moscow is using arms sales as a political instrument in its foreign policy.
(Through translator) Russia is always looking for trump cards in its aims against the West.
Special relations with so-called pariah countries is one of those cards.
Washington recently enacted sanctions against Rosoboron Export and other Russian arms companies in a growing standoff that looks set to worsen strained relations between the two countries.
Gregory Feifer, NPR News, Moscow.
The State Department's Iraq Team is going through some changes.
Secretary of State Condoleezza Rice formerly announced today that the ambassador to Iraq, Zalmay Khalilzad, will return to the U.S.
He will become, if confirmed, America's ambassador to the United Nations.
The Bush administration is sending Ryan Crocker, a seasoned diplomat, to Baghdad.
NPR's Michele Kelemen has that story,
Just two days before President Bush is due to announce his new strategy for Iraq, Secretary Rice announced her new team and pledged, as she put it, unwavering support for them.
If confirmed, Ryan and Zal will have two of the hardest and most consequential jobs in the world.
But President Bush and I have the outmost confidence that they are more than equal to the challenges of their post.
Zalmay Khalilzad has been a central figure in the Bush administration.
Born in Afghanistan, he's a Sunni Muslim who speaks several languages, including Arabic, and he prides himself on his negotiating skills.
As he told NPR last year from the embassy in Baghdad, he can drink as much tea or coffee as anyone.
(Soundbite of archived interview)
Here, the concept of time is not the same.
And I remember from Afghanistan when dealing with the Taliban, they used to say while we had all the watches, they had all the time.
And I've been telling Washington and the Americans there that this is the defining challenge of our time.
We got to succeed, but we have to understand how difficult and complicated the issues are, and that that we need to be patient as well.
Well, I mean our view on - from the editorial page perspective, we haven't actually weighed on the march itself, just in the context of this particular issue.

We note that this particular administration, Mayor Bloomberg, and this police commissioner, Ray Kelly, already have some of the highest amounts of communication between city hall and the black community that has been in New York in decades, probably.
This is not like - one area where this is very, very different from - the Amadou Diallo situation was there you had Rudy Giuliani, who refused to sit down with any black leaders.
Not just Al Sharpton types, but elected black leaders like the Manhattan Borough president at the time, Virginia Fields.
And Howard Safir, the police commissioner was, you know, kind of similarly also -similarly disengaged.
I'm not quite so sure if this particular kind of a rally is going to have -necessarily have that kind of an impact aside from the idea of this kind of a therapeutic for the community itself.
Because right now the basic process is going on.
The judicial process is going on.
You do have the Queens DA, Richard Brown, looking at the details of it and he will, you know, make his decision, you know, within the next few weeks.
So I'm not quite so sure whether - I mean it was certainly powerful symbolically, but I don't really think this kind of a rally is going to, say, influence the judicial process.
I want to move on to Senator Obama.
There's some question as to whether or not he's going to run for president in 2008.
But some people are saying go for it, dude.
You know, they've already drafted him.
Draft Obama is an all-volunteer organization not affiliated with the senator and they're premiering an ad.
(Soundbite of political ad)
Do we participate in the politics of cynicism, or do we participate in a politics of hope?
We believe in making sure that everybody should have the opportunity to get a job that pays a living wage.
That's something we believe in.
And we spoke with Ben Stanfield, who's the founder of DraftObama.org, about Obama's potential as a candidate.
I think it's his ability to transcend almost everything about our politics in America today.
You know, he has the ability, the unique ability for a Democrat, to reach out to people like evangelical Christians, to reach across racial lines with, you know, his black father and white mother.
He has the ability to reach across economic lines and liberal, conservative, Democrat, Republicans.
He has the ability to transcend that and just spread that message of hope that he talks about so much in his books from his speaking.
Robert, I want to start singing, we are the world, we are the children.
Kumbaya, my friends.
Kumbaya.
So what are we to make of this, you know, outside campaigning?
I think it is kind of nice that there is sort of a kind of a feel-good story about politics.

Though, I mean I think I can speak for all journalists when I say that the first answer, the first answer to Mr. Obama's question is, I'm sorry but the politics of cynicism will actually end up trumping the politics of hope because…
(Soundbite of laughter)

Pardon my cynicism, but that's kind of the way that the system works because, you know, it's very - I think it is very true that he is certainly trying to work with evangelicals, you know, cross certain kinds of lines.
But what happens is you end up - the process ends up kind of weeding out this kind of feel-goodness and start looking at basic Brass - Brass Tacks, T-A-C-K-S -issues.
And when it comes to, let's say, like he is supportive of, for one example, you know, late term abortions, partial birth abortions.
That is automatically going to cleave away at a certain segment of the populous right there.
And you're going to end up getting to a position where the issues will start transcending even his own personal great charisma, which is dramatic.
We're talking to Robert George.
He's an editorial writer at the New York Post.
Of course, this is NEWS & NOTES and I'm Farai Chideya.
And we also have Marcelo Suarez-Orozco, professor of immigration studies at New York University.
In Boston, Callie Crossley, social and cultural commentator on the TV show "Beat the Press.
So Callie - let me go to you next - you know, it's really nice to have other people making your ambitions happen.
To what degree is this significant that other people are really trying to carry the Obama banner?
MS.
CROSSLEY: I don't - well, here's what's significant about it to me, is that remember, we may have forgotten, there is a Draft Condi movement too.
She's dismissed it many times but it still exists in terms of getting to her to think about running for office.

So I think any time you have these outside groups trying to foment some interest and stir up some interest and boost the candidate, it's something that the potential candidate has to pay attention to.

I think what's interesting to me is that we have two black people for the first time having outside groups trying to, you know, get them to consider running for the highest office in the land, however real that may be.
Which just goes to prove that the draft attacked - the draft impacts black people more than it does white people.
(Soundbite of laughter)
